questions.text: When someone I care about is hurting, my motivation to help remains high until I know they are better.
I approach conflict by focusing on outcomes rather than on the emotions involved.
I have changed my plans because I became aware that someone needed help, even when it was inconvenient.
I think that most emotional reactions to others' suffering are disproportionate to what is actually needed.
When I am near someone in deep pain, their suffering exists in my awareness as a moral weight that requires a response.
I can evaluate a person's difficult situation without my feelings about that person influencing my analysis.
I believe truly helping someone requires you to understand their experience from their perspective.
I have noticed that I recover from exposure to others' suffering faster than most people around me.
I find that I cannot simply turn off my concern for someone once I have seen their pain.
I believe that emotional clarity comes from being an observer of feelings rather than a participant in them.
I take it personally when I learn that someone in my life has been suffering alone.
I have been able to set aside sympathy for an individual to make a decision that served a larger group.
I feel that the depth of my caring for others is something I would never want to diminish.
I am more likely to ask what happened than how do you feel when someone is upset.
I believe that emotional connection to others' suffering is what drives people to make the world better.
I think of empathy as a tool that should be deployed strategically rather than experienced reflexively.
I am often the person others come to when they need someone who will truly feel what they are going through.
I value clarity of thought over depth of feeling when making important decisions that affect others.
